source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)
revision_note: Newly added with 8 verified chunks under public domain (2026-07-23).

content.text: Chanakya (Kautilya) was a teacher at Taxila and counselor to Chandragupta Maurya, seeking to unite the divided kingdoms of India under a single righteous administration.
content.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

content.text: After being insulted by the Nanda king, Chanakya untied his topknot (shikha), vowing to keep it untied until he had destroyed the corrupt dynasty and established a just rule under Chandragupta.
content.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

content.text: Chanakya selected the young Chandragupta, trained him in martial arts and statecraft, and navigated immense political intrigues to guide him to the Maurya throne.
content.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

content.text: Though serving as the prime minister of a vast empire, Chanakya lived in a humble mud hut, stating that a ruler's wealth lies in the prosperity of his subjects, not in personal luxury.
content.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

content.text: "In the happiness of his subjects lies the king's happiness; in their welfare his welfare. What pleases himself he shall not consider as good, but whatever pleases his subjects he shall consider as good." (Rajadharma, Book I, Chapter XIX).
content.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

content.text: "Just as it is impossible not to taste honey or poison that finds itself on the tongue, so it is impossible for a government servant not to eat up at least a bit of the king's revenue," advising strict accountability.
content.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

content.text: Chanakya's guidance led to the creation of the Maurya Empire, which brought peace, unity, and prosperity across the subcontinent, and his writings remain a classic on ethical governance.
content.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)

content.text: This entry compiles the translation from R. Shamasastry's "Kautilya's Arthashastra" (1915), which is in the public domain.
content.source_name: Kautilya's Arthashastra, translated by R. Shamasastry (1915)
